preFix.title: Calculating off-grid electrical load requirements
preFix.subtitle: Sizing a battery bank and solar array from your actual daily consumption
preFix.excerpt: An off-grid electrical system must be sized to cover the worst-case daily energy demand, not the average, because a battery bank running short on a January week of low sun and high demand is the design failure condition. A systematic load calculation worksheet identifies each appliance, its wattage, and its daily hours of use, to produce a realistic daily energy demand figure that drives the battery and solar array sizing.
preFix.sourceNotes: Victron Energy off-grid system design guide 2024. Rolls Battery off-grid sizing documentation. Paul Wheaton, Off-Grid Solar (2019). Renewable UK off-grid residential case studies.

content.text: Sizing an off-grid system for average demand gives a system that runs short in bad weather. The right approach is to size for peak demand over 2 to 3 days of low sun in a row. In a UK location, this means planning for winter conditions with a run of overcast days.
For each item, write down three things: the wattage, how many hours it runs per day in winter. And whether it can be switched off in bad weather.
Common off-grid loads and approximate wattages:.
LED lighting: 5 to 15 W; 4 to 6 hours/day = 20 to 90 Wh/day.
Laptop: 45 to 65 W; 8 hours/day = 360 to 520 Wh/day.
Refrigerator: 35 to 50 W average running; continuous = 840 to 1200 Wh/day.
Phone charging: 5 to 18 W; 2 hours/day = 10 to 36 Wh/day.

Sum the Wh/day figures for all loads. Add a 15% inefficiency margin for inverter and wiring losses. This gives the design daily demand in Wh. A simple off-grid cabin with essential-only loads typically runs at 1,500 to 2,500 Wh/day.
Battery capacity = Daily demand × Autonomy days ÷ Max depth of discharge.
For a UK cabin with 2 kWh/day demand, 3 days of autonomy. And lithium iron phosphate batteries at 80% DoD: capacity = 2.0 × 3 ÷ 0.80 = 7.5 kWh.
Array size = Daily demand ÷ peak sun hours ÷ System efficiency.

For 2 kWh/day in Yorkshire. Winter 2.0 peak sun hours: 2.0 ÷ 2.0 ÷ 0.8 = 1.25 kWp minimum. In practice, size at 1.5× minimum to allow for panel degradation and sub-optimal orientation. At 400 W per panel, that is 5 panels.
Step-by-step instructions for Calculating off-grid electrical load requirements go here.
